summary:
The Jin Dynasty (265–420 AD) briefly reunified China after the [[event:2046|Three Kingdoms Period]] before collapsing in one of Chinese history's most catastrophic political implosions. Founded by Sima Yan (Emperor Wu), who completed conquest of the Wu kingdom in 280, Jin reunification proved short-lived. A succession crisis triggered the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 AD) — a devastating civil war among members of the Sima clan — which so weakened the dynasty that nomadic peoples from the north were able to invade and overrun the north China plain.

The Uprising of the Five Barbarians (Wuhu) — the Xiongnu, Jie, Xianbei, Di, and Qiang peoples — led to the sack of Luoyang in 311 and Chang'an in 316, killing or capturing the Jin emperor and ending the Western Jin dynasty. The Jin court fled south across the Yangtze River and reestablished as the Eastern Jin dynasty (317–420 AD) in Jiankang (modern Nanjing), ruling southern China while the north fractured into a kaleidoscope of short-lived nomadic states (the Sixteen Kingdoms period). This north-south division — with a sophisticated Chinese court culture in the south and shifting nomadic states in the north — persisted for nearly three centuries until reunification under the Sui dynasty in 589. The period profoundly shaped Chinese identity, Buddhism's spread in China, and the cultural development of southern China.
